title: Entire Building Slows to a Crawl After Conference Room AV Cart Reconnected

ticket.summary: Starting at 10:14 this morning, users across multiple floors and multiple VLANs (Sales VLAN 10, Engineering VLAN 20, Guest VLAN 50) report the network becoming unusably slow — web pages timing out, VoIP calls dropping, RDP sessions freezing. Symptoms are building-wide, not isolated to one floor. AV support says they reconnected a conference room presentation cart in Room 3B around the same time after cleaning up cables.

topology.description: Room 3B has a wall plate with two jacks (3B-A and 3B-B) that both terminate on access switch sw-fl3-acc2, intended for the presentation cart's laptop input and a secondary display uplink respectively. Both jacks are enabled access ports on VLAN 10. sw-fl3-acc2 uplinks to distribution switch sw-fl3-dist1, which uplinks to core switch sw-core1; sw-core1 interconnects all floor distribution switches. After cable cleanup, the AV cart's single patch cable was mistakenly run from jack 3B-A back into jack 3B-B on the same wall plate, looping both switch ports together on the same switch and same VLAN.

links.label: loop: jack 3B-A to jack 3B-B, both on Gi0/9 and Gi0/10

monitoring.notes: Building-wide broadcast packets-per-second spiked from a baseline of ~200 pps to over 480,000 pps at 10:14, holding steady until manual intervention. CPU utilization crossed 90% on sw-core1, sw-fl1-acc1, sw-fl2-acc1, and sw-fl3-dist1 within 15 seconds of each other, radiating outward from sw-fl3-acc2 which spiked first at 10:14:11 — consistent with a loop originating there and flooding across the trunked core.

hints: The outage is building-wide across multiple VLANs and switches, and CPU spiked almost simultaneously everywhere — look for a single originating point rather than treating this as several unrelated issues.
sw-fl3-acc2 shows the earliest timestamp for the spike, and its MAC address table log shows the same MAC address flapping rapidly between two ports on the same switch.

Ports Gi0/9 and Gi0/10 on sw-fl3-acc2 (the two jacks for the Room 3B AV cart) are looped together by a single patch cable, and neither port has BPDU Guard configured, so STP portfast edge ports didn't block the loop before the broadcast storm formed.

solution.root_cause: AV support ran a single patch cable from jack 3B-A back into jack 3B-B on the same wall plate in Room 3B, physically looping switch ports Gi0/9 and Gi0/10 on sw-fl3-acc2 together on the same VLAN. Both ports were configured with spanning-tree portfast for fast AV device connectivity but without BPDU Guard, so the loop was not blocked and produced a broadcast storm that flooded outward through the trunked uplinks to sw-fl3-dist1, sw-core1, and every distribution switch in the building.
solution.fix: Disconnect the looped patch cable between jacks 3B-A and 3B-B on sw-fl3-acc2, then enable BPDU Guard (or disable portfast) on Gi0/9 and Gi0/10 so any future accidental loop on those ports is shut down automatically instead of flooding the network.
